Course Title: AI in Logistics Optimisation: When Efficiency Backfires
Category: Transportation & Logistics: Aviation, Maritime, Logistics, Shipping, Automotive
Description: Trade-offs between speed, cost, and resilience.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with logistics or supply chain concepts, along with a general curiosity about how artificial intelligence is being applied in real-world operations, is all you need to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key trade-offs between speed, cost, and resilience when applying AI to logistics optimisation, and explain how over-reliance on efficiency-driven algorithms can introduce fragility into supply chains. You will also apply practical frameworks to evaluate when AI-driven optimisation genuinely improves logistics performance and when it risks backfiring in real-world transportation and shipping contexts.

lessons.lesson title: AI in Logistics Optimisation: When Efficiency Backfires
lessons.Description: Artificial intelligence is transforming logistics, helping companies move goods faster, cut costs, and streamline complex supply chains. But optimising purely for efficiency can create hidden vulnerabilities, leaving businesses exposed when disruptions strike. In this lesson, you will explore real-world examples of how AI-driven logistics decisions have backfired, and why chasing speed or cost savings does not always lead to the best outcomes. You will learn to recognise the trade-offs between efficiency, cost, and resilience, and begin to think critically about how AI tools should be applied in logistics contexts. By the end, you will have a clearer understanding of how to balance the benefits of AI optimisation with the need for robust, adaptable supply chains.
lessons.content: Trade-offs between speed, cost, and resilience.

slides.subtitle: When speed costs more than time.
slides.slidecontent:
• Faster isn't always better
• Efficiency creates fragility
• Speed, cost, resilience clash
• Small gains, big risks
• Trade-offs matter enormously

slides.subtitle: How AI strips away protective buffers
slides.slidecontent:
• AI removes 'slack' to minimise cost or maximise speed
• No buffer means no recovery options
• Single disruptions cascade across networks
• Optimised routes leave no room for rerouting
• Systems perform brilliantly until pressure hits

slides.subtitle: The hidden price of cutting corners
slides.slidecontent:
• Cost cuts eliminate backup options
• Single suppliers become weak points
• Spare capacity gets flagged as waste
• Disruptions spread quickly downstream
• Resilience requires deliberate investment

slides.subtitle: When faster systems lose adaptability
slides.slidecontent:
• Faster systems have less flexibility
• Tight connections spread problems rapidly
• No hard shoulder means no recovery space
• Single points of failure multiply risk
• Speed only works if systems survive disruption

slides.subtitle: Trading small problems for big disasters
slides.slidecontent:
• Hours to cascade from one delay to system-wide failure
• Redundancy feels wasteful until desperately needed
• Perfect conditions don't last in real operations
• Small manageable problems become rare catastrophic ones
• Brittleness hidden until the breaking point

slides.subtitle: The advantage of deliberate inefficiency
slides.slidecontent:
• Backup routes provide escape valves
• Buffer stock absorbs demand spikes
• Extra capacity handles unexpected loads
• Slower systems bend rather than break
• Redundancy is insurance, not waste

slides.subtitle: Recovery strategies when systems break down
slides.slidecontent:
• Diversify suppliers immediately
• Build manual backup processes
• Restock safety stock buffers
• Communicate delays proactively
• Review single points of failure

slides.subtitle: Core lessons for smarter AI decisions
slides.slidecontent:
• AI targets efficiency at cost of resilience
• Speed and cost savings mask fragility
• Build slack into logistics design
• Small buffers prevent large failures
• Audit where systems cannot adapt

scripts.script: Welcome, everyone, and thank you for joining this course. We're going to be exploring something that might seem a little counterintuitive at first: the idea that making your logistics operations faster and more efficient can actually make them more vulnerable. Now, that probably sounds strange. Surely faster and cheaper is always the goal, right? Well, not quite. When we push hard for efficiency, we often strip away the slack, the buffers, and the flexibility that keep a supply chain healthy when things go wrong. And things do go wrong. What we end up with is a system that performs brilliantly under perfect conditions but struggles badly the moment it faces any kind of disruption. Throughout this lesson, we'll be looking at how speed, cost, and resilience can pull in very different directions, and why the small gains we chase can sometimes carry surprisingly large risks. The trade-offs here really do matter, so let's dig in.

So here's the core problem we're going to keep coming back to throughout this course. When AI is given a single goal, say, cut costs or speed up delivery, it gets very good at stripping out anything it sees as waste. And that includes the buffers, the spare capacity, the little bits of wiggle room that human planners often build in almost instinctively. The trouble is, that wiggle room isn't actually waste. It's what keeps the whole system stable when something goes wrong. And things do go wrong. A shipment gets delayed, a port closes unexpectedly, a supplier misses a deadline. In a well-optimised system, there's often no slack left to absorb that kind of disruption, so it ripples outward and affects everything else. The system looks brilliant on a normal day, but it becomes surprisingly fragile the moment conditions change.

Now let's look specifically at what happens when AI focuses on cutting costs. The algorithm sees multiple suppliers and thinks, why pay three when one will do? It sees spare warehouse capacity and flags it as unnecessary expense. It looks at buffer stock sitting on shelves and calculates how much money is tied up doing nothing. On paper, this all makes perfect sense. But in reality, you're creating single points of failure throughout your network. If that one cheaper supplier has a problem, your entire operation feels it immediately. And because modern supply chains are so interconnected, a cost-cutting decision in one area can create vulnerabilities that show up somewhere completely different when things go wrong.

Here's where the speed obsession gets particularly dangerous. When AI optimises for the fastest possible delivery, it creates what we might call a motorway with no hard shoulder. Everything flows beautifully until there's an incident, and then suddenly there's nowhere to go. Fast systems are tight systems, and tight systems don't adapt well. A delayed shipment that might have been a minor inconvenience in a looser network becomes a major problem because there are no alternative routes, no spare capacity to absorb the delay. The connections between different parts of the supply chain are so direct and immediate that problems spread at the same speed as the goods themselves.

What we're really looking at here is a specific type of failure pattern. Instead of the small, frequent hiccups that human-managed systems used to have, AI-optimised networks tend to run smoothly for long periods and then fail catastrophically when something finally goes wrong. Think of it like the difference between a car that needs regular minor repairs versus one that runs perfectly until the engine seizes completely. The tightly connected nature of these systems means that when one thing breaks, the problem spreads incredibly quickly. What starts as a single delayed shipment can cascade across an entire supply network within hours, because there are no circuit breakers, no places where the system can absorb the disruption and prevent it from spreading further.

This is why slightly looser, less optimised systems often handle real-world conditions better than their highly tuned counterparts. When you have backup suppliers, alternative routes, and a bit of extra stock sitting around, you have options when things go wrong. These things look inefficient from a pure cost perspective, but they're actually sophisticated risk management tools. A system with some redundancy built in can bend under pressure rather than break completely. It might not deliver the absolute best performance on a perfect day, but it keeps functioning when conditions become challenging. And in the real world, conditions become challenging fairly regularly.

So when a highly optimised system breaks down, what do you actually do next? The first priority is to stop relying on a single source for anything critical. Start reaching out to alternative suppliers straight away, even if they cost a little more. At the same time, make sure your team knows how to operate without the automated tools. Those manual backup processes might feel old-fashioned, but they are genuinely lifesaving in a crisis. You will also want to rebuild your safety stock buffers as quickly as possible, because lean inventories leave you very exposed during recovery. Whilst all of this is happening, keep your customers and partners informed. Proactive communication builds trust, even when the news is not great. And finally, once the dust settles, sit down and honestly identify where your single points of failure are.

So let's bring this all together. The central idea here is that AI optimisation is genuinely powerful, but it always involves a trade-off. When you chase efficiency, you are usually giving up resilience or flexibility in return, and the danger is that you often do not notice until something goes wrong. Fast, lean systems can look brilliant on paper, but they can hide real fragility underneath. When disruptions hit, and they will, over-optimised systems tend to buckle quickly. So the practical lesson is this: build some slack into your logistics design. That might mean holding a small buffer of stock, or keeping a secondary supplier on your list, even if you rarely use them. These things feel inefficient, but they are actually your safety net. And a good place to start is simply auditing your current setup and asking honestly: where does this system have absolutely no room to adapt?

Who is this for: Supply chain managers, logistics coordinators, operations managers, transportation planners, warehouse supervisors, procurement specialists, supply chain analysts
Team or Dept this is for: Supply Chain, Logistics, Operations, Transportation, Procurement, Warehouse Operations
